text.title: sql-optimization Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for sql-optimization. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Always run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT) before adding any index or rewriting any query.
rule.rationale: The planner's chosen plan often differs from developer expectations. Optimization without measurement produces indexes that are never used and rewrites that change nothing.

rule.statement: Investigate when actual rows differ from estimated rows by more than 10x — this signals stale statistics.
rule.rationale: The planner chooses join order and index use based on row estimates. Large discrepancies cause poor plan choices that cannot be fixed by indexes alone; run ANALYZE first.

rule.statement: In composite indexes, place equality-filter columns first, range-filter columns last.
rule.rationale: The B-tree can use prefix columns for equality and then traverse a range on the last column. Placing range columns first makes the index unusable for the equality filter.

rule.statement: Use INCLUDE columns to add non-filtered columns to an index to avoid heap lookups for read-heavy queries.
rule.rationale: A covering index satisfies the query entirely from index pages, eliminating the heap fetch (table lookup) for each matched row.

rule.statement: Use partial indexes with a WHERE clause for queries that always filter on a specific value.
rule.rationale: Partial indexes are smaller (fewer rows indexed) and faster to scan than full indexes. Useful for status columns where only one value (e.g. 'pending') is queried frequently.

rule.statement: Create an expression index when queries apply a function to an indexed column; do not apply functions to indexed columns in WHERE clauses.
rule.rationale: WHERE LOWER(email) = ? bypasses the regular index on email. An expression index on LOWER(email) makes the query use the index.

rule.statement: Use keyset (cursor) pagination instead of OFFSET for any collection larger than ~1000 rows.
rule.rationale: OFFSET N scans and discards N rows on every page. At page 500 with 20 items per page, the database scans 10,000 rows to return 20. Keyset pagination uses an index seek regardless of page depth.

rule.statement: Replace subqueries in SELECT lists with a single JOIN plus aggregation to eliminate N+1 query patterns.
rule.rationale: A subquery in the SELECT list executes once per row in the outer result. 1000 rows = 1001 queries. A JOIN executes once regardless of result set size.

rule.statement: Use multi-row INSERT for bulk inserts; use CTE with LIMIT for large batch updates to avoid long-running transactions.
rule.rationale: Individual INSERT per row in a loop causes N round trips and N transaction commits. A batched UPDATE without LIMIT locks all matching rows for the full duration.

rule.statement: Use materialized views for expensive aggregations queried frequently; refresh CONCURRENTLY to avoid blocking reads.
rule.rationale: Re-computing GROUP BY over millions of rows on every dashboard load is wasteful. A materialized view precomputes it; CONCURRENTLY refresh avoids a read lock on the view.
